title: practical english usage - Part 208
content:
Inversion (putting the verb before the subject: ►    271) is not only used for People like that I just can't stand. He's a funny guy, old Fred.

This question we have already discussed.

there, this can draw extra attention to it. Fronting (►  272, 299.3) has this effect.

If we  move to the end of a sentence something that does not normally belong inversion  and fronting 5  changing       the   order: I'm not sure what is the point of spending hours and hours discussing this.

I'm not sure what the point is.

End-weight can also affect the word order of indirect questions. Compare: that you can't.

She plays very well almost any instrument that you can think of and several not She plays the violin very well (     She plays very weU the violin.) shorter adverbial. Compare: clause (►   196.1). However, a very long and heavy object may come after a Adverbials do not normally separate the verb from the object in an English uw-HOt-bHhedeastinierrslrddatt.) not He made it clear that he was not in the least interested, (     He-made-thaf-he everything you know is important.) It's important to tell us everything you know. (More natural than To tell us That she hadn't been in touch for so long worried me.) It worried me that she hadn't been in touch for so long. (More natural than clause subject or object to the end of a sentence. For details, ►    268-269.

Because of this, we  often use a structure with 'preparatory it’ in order to move a a musical instrument sometimes discourages children.) musical instrument. (More natural than The length of time it takes to learn Children are sometimes discouraged by the length of time it takes to learn a (These usually have the highest 'information-value' in any case.) Longer and heavier structures usually come last in a clause or sentence.

4 end-weight:       It worried         me    that     she    hadn't      been      in   touch.

There are children all over the house.) I've got the house full of children, (instead of The house is full of children.

person the subject.

Structures with have are often used to ‘ personalise’  a situation by making a Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  268  preparatory it: subject But it looks as though we may have to.

It will be a pity if we have to ask her to leave.

It's not as if this was the first time she's been difficult.

It looks as if we're going to have trouble with Anna again.

It is used to introduce some clauses with if, as if and as though.

5 if,  as   if  and   as   though How long does it take to get to London from here?

It took me months to get to know her.

is necessary (►  602).

Note the common use of this structure to say how  much time 4 It takes      ...  + infinitive For there as a preparatory subject with any/no use, ►    20.2.

For more information about structures with worth, ►    634.

It surprised me you not remembering my name.

It's no use trying to explain - I'm not interested.

It's worth going to Wales if you have the time.

It was nice seeing you.  It's crazy her going off like that.

It can be a preparatory subject for an -ing form. This is usually informal.

3   It was     nice     seeing       you.

It's exciting when a baby starts talking.

It's surprising how many unhappy marriages there are.

It doesn 't interest me what you think.

It's probable that we'll be a little late.

beginning with that, what, how, etc.

We   also use preparatory ft when the subject of a clause is itself a clause 2  It's  probable          that     we'll     be   late.

It's essential for the papers to be ready before Thursday.

It will suit me best for you to arrive at about ten o'clock.

It can also be a preparatory subject for for + object + infinitive (►   113).

It upsets me to hear people arguing all the time.

It was a pleasure to listen to her.

It's my ambition to run a three-hour marathon.

It's important to reserve in advance.

It was stupid of you to leave the door unlocked.

It was good of you to phone.

It's nice to talk to you. (More natural than To talk to you is nice.) be 4- adjective/noun.

put towards the end of a sentence, ►    267). Preparatory it is common before it, and to put the infinitive expression later (long or complicated items are often come at the beginning. We   usually prefer to start with the ‘ preparatory subject' When the subject of a clause is an infinitive expression, this does not normally 1  It's  nice     to   talk     to   you.
